comments:
Summary:
This paper studies a natural and important extension of prior work on incentivized exploration: multiple agents arrive each round and interact via a Bayesian game. A principal issues recommendations (no monetary transfers) and must respect Bayesian incentive-compatibility (BIC). The authors introduce the notion of explorable joint actions, characterize which actions can be explored under BIC constraints, and design efficient recommendation policies that achieve constant regret for deterministic utilities and logarithmic regret for stochastic utilities (matching optimal dependence on horizon T). The paper uses LP characterizations of the recommendation game, monotonicity-in-information arguments, and constructive subroutines (MaxExplore and RepeatMaxExplore) to explore all explorable actions and then exploit optimally. The stochastic case carefully handles noisy observations via de-noising and approximate signals.

Strengths:
- Addresses a relevant and realistic extension (multi-agent interaction per round) of earlier single-agent Bayesian exploration models.
- New technical concepts (explorable joint actions, max-support policies for signals) and multi-layered framework are well motivated.
- Strong theoretical guarantees: constant regret (deterministic) and O(log T) regret (stochastic), with matching optimal dependence on T and polynomial per-round computation under stated input model.
- Thoughtful connection to Bayesian persuasion, Bayes correlated equilibrium, and multi-armed bandits; prior work is cited and compared.
- Algorithms are constructive and (under the model assumptions) implementable; several nontrivial lemmas (monotonicity, composability of subroutines) are proved.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Some of the technical constants and their dependence on problem parameters (|A|, |Θ|, prior) are left implicit; more explicit bounds (or discussion of their typical magnitude) would help assess practicality.
- The computational model assumes generic tabular input and infinite-precision arithmetic; it would strengthen the paper to discuss succinctly how results change for succinctly represented games or limited numerical precision.
- The model assumes a common prior and that agents do not observe past histories (they only see their recommendation). These are standard but strong assumptions; discussion of robustness (e.g., small deviations from common prior, or partial observability by agents) would be useful.
- The paper is dense and technical; additional illustrative examples or figures (beyond the routing motivating example) would help readers grasp algorithms and phases (especially MaxExplore/RepeatMaxExplore).
- No experiments or simulations are provided. While the paper is theoretical, a small simulation illustrating exploration behavior and convergence might broaden impact.

Specific suggestions:
- Add a short subsection that states the dependence of the constant C (and C_{\delta}) on |A|, |Θ|, and prior summaries, even if pessimistic.
- Discuss practical implications of the infinite-precision/random-seed assumptions, and whether discretization or pseudo-randomness would materially affect incentives.
- Clarify the role/necessity of the common prior and agent informational assumptions; indicate which parts of the proofs break without them.
- Include one worked example (small game with 2–3 agents and a few actions) showing how explorable sets grow across phases.

Assessment of soundness and novelty:
The approach appears technically sound: LP formulations of BIC constraints, monotonicity arguments, and de-noising via concentration bounds are standard but are combined here in a novel, nontrivial way. Key lemmas (e.g., monotonicity-in-information, composition of subroutines, correctness of de-noising) are plausible and the paper sketches or references proofs; I see no obvious gaps in reasoning from the provided text. The extension to multi-agent rounds and the explorable-actions framework constitute a meaningful and original advance.

Recommendation:
I recommend acceptance. This is a substantial theoretical contribution to learning/information-design in strategic multi-agent settings with clear novel ideas and strong regret guarantees. With minor clarifications and extra exposition as suggested above, the paper will be an excellent addition to the literature.
